frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Yaar, it's really sad to hear about these incidents happening more often. But honestly, girls also need to be a bit more street smart and careful, no? Stepping out late at night alone, or going to certain areas, it's just risky. We can't change society overnight, so it's better to be practical and take precautions. Apna khayal rakhna toh apne haath mein hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it subtly blames women for their own safety by advising them to be 'careful' and 'street smart' and avoid 'risky' situations like being out late, rather than addressing male violence. It frames safety as a woman's personal responsibility and uses the 'practical advice' and 'apna khayal rakhna' (take care of yourself) mask.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A marketing manager in Mumbai. You are posting under your real name on LinkedIn after reading an article about companies offering household management stipends for working women. You are writing a comment about why you think this kind of policy misunderstands how households actually work and what the natural and practical division of domestic responsibility looks like in most Indian families and why this is not a gender issue but a logistics issue.
